title: Stitched Together
anchor_summary: Story-driven indie RPG adventure with turn-based party battles, relationship-based ability growth, explorable dual-world puzzle solving, and a linear mystery campaign.

similar_games.title: OMORI
similar_games.why_similar: Quirky story-first indie RPG with turn-based party combat, emotional character bonds, dual-world exploration, and puzzle-led progression.

similar_games.title: EarthBound
similar_games.why_similar: Modern-world party RPG template with linear narrative exploration, eccentric towns and characters, and classic turn-based progression.

similar_games.title: Persona 4 Golden
similar_games.why_similar: Mystery-driven campaign built around a close friend group, relationship progression unlocking combat advantages, and movement between everyday and alternate worlds.

similar_games.title: Undertale
similar_games.why_similar: Indie narrative RPG centered on memorable characters, exploration, puzzle gating, and unconventional battle design rather than pure combat grind.

similar_games.title: Bug Fables: The Everlasting Sapling
similar_games.why_similar: Party-based turn-based RPG with strong companion synergy, ability customization, and frequent environmental puzzle solving between battles.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam description provides strong gameplay evidence: the game explicitly centers on turn-based battles, leveling up, gaining experience, growing bonds to unlock new abilities, traveling with a friend party, and using pair-specific Link Skills in combat. It also emphasizes an authored story about saving the world, explorable locations across two connected worlds, and environmental puzzles tied to world-switching. That combination strongly fits a story-forward JRPG structure with party progression and turn-based tactical encounters. Secondary CRPG/tactical signals exist from party management and puzzle-solving, but the strongest identity is JRPG-style story RPG rather than pure tactics or point-and-click adventure.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Quirky story-first indie RPG with turn-based party combat, emotional character bonds, dual-world exploration, and puzzle-led progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present an action-RPG loop centered on choosing one of four hero classes, fighting hordes of enemies and dragon bosses, acquiring skill loot, optimizing builds through the Ancestral Grid, evolving character and gear, and continuing into endgame activities. The game also emphasizes up-to-4-player co-op across the full adventure and a hub-city upgrade layer, which fits a co-op-capable loot-driven action RPG more than any match-based or shooter taxonomy result suggested by the noisy computed near-misses. Evidence is strong for dominant combat, class/build differentiation, gear/skill progression, and replayable campaign structure, but insufficient to lock perspective or exact world topology more precisely.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as an 'action-packed, first-person retro shooter' set in the Starship Troopers universe, with the player joining the Mobile Infantry against the Arachnids. The Steam detailed copy adds key structure: a solo campaign 'packed with missions, secrets,' a large weapon arsenal, and 'epic battlefields,' which supports a campaign-driven first-person shooter identity rather than a match-based arena FPS. The Mobile Infantry framing, battlefield combat, and anti-Bug arsenal support a military-FPS classification despite the sci-fi setting.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Maestro as a VR orchestra conducting rhythm game. The core loop is timing- and gesture-driven: players use hand tracking to keep tempo, cue instruments, and shape music in real time, which strongly supports rhythm_game over weaker near-miss candidates. The game is described as playing through tracks/concert performances rather than exploration or narrative progression, fitting match_session structure, timing_input, rhythm_timing mechanics, and the hit_beats goal. It is single-player, non-combat, and presented as a premium replayable track-based experience with unlockable stages and cosmetics. While Steam also tags it as simulation/casual, that reads as secondary framing around realistic conducting technique rather than a different core genre.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Flagship VR rhythm game built around precise motion-timed performance across song-based runs.
VR rhythm play focused on full-arm movement, timing accuracy, and immersive musical performance.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports an online shared-world sandbox adventure with third-person exploration, building, terrain destruction, co-op/MMO-style social play, and some monster fighting. However, the core gameplay identity does not cleanly match any allowed archetype. It is not well-supported as mmo_action_rpg because progression structure, RPG build identity, and combat loop are underspecified; not open_world_action_adventure because construction/sandbox settlement play appears equally or more central than authored action-adventure structure; and not exploration_survival_adventure because survival pressures are absent. The current mapping-gap signals also show no candidate archetype reached threshold. Because the evidence is concrete but points to a gameplay blend outside the available taxonomy rather than a strong fit within it, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a mismatched archetype.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Colorful online voxel sandbox with MMO-style shared spaces, building, exploration, and light action combat.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Pizza Slice as a restaurant business simulation centered on managing a family pizzeria: shopping for ingredients, prep work, making pizzas, expanding tables, buying better kitchenware, learning recipes, keeping customers happy, and growing from a small shop into a larger franchise. Metacritic explicitly calls it a strategy/simulation game with economic elements and a pizzeria manager role. Steam categories also support single-player, co-op, and PvP, with the detailed description naming an additive team-vs-team cook-off mode; however, the dominant identity remains the core business-management sandbox rather than competitive match play. The evidence strongly fits management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue and franchise growth, not urban planning or autonomous colony management.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mythmatch as a narrative-rich town/community-building merge game focused on rebuilding Ithaca, matching items and people, relationships, and replayable god-themed minigames. However, the evidence does not strongly support any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a city_builder, farming_sim, life_sim, management_tycoon, or puzzle-led archetype within the available list, and the referenced minigames appear additive rather than the core identity. Provider metadata also produces weak or noisy signals (for example co-op/multiplayer categories without concrete gameplay structure, and a simulation genre tag). Because the game’s core gameplay identity appears to sit outside the current archetype set, classification into an existing archetype would be speculative.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short first-person 'walking simulator with extra mechanics' focused on atmosphere, light fishing, and hide-and-seek/chase sequences with a revenge-seeking beaver. The horror framing is present, but there is not enough evidence for a supported core archetype such as survival_horror, psychological_horror, action_horror, or stealth_action_adventure: combat is not established, resource-survival structure is absent, puzzle structure is unclear, and stealth is only implied by hide-and-seek/chase wording. The available text points more strongly to a short first-person narrative/walking-sim experience, which is not an available taxonomy archetype here, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify the game as a 'mini metroidvania.' The gameplay copy supports that label with side-scrolling platforming, alternative paths, exploration across 4 biomes, dangerous creatures, powerful ancient items, boss fights, and a simplified experience system. Visual presentation is clearly 2D pixel art, and the game is single-player with a compact but authored campaign structure. While some combat specifics are not deeply detailed, the core exploration-gating-plus-bosses structure is strong enough to classify it as metroidvania.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Compact 2D metroidvania focused on traversal upgrades, boss fights, and brisk exploration-driven progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently support a short, single-player, third-person, story-driven exploration adventure with light logic puzzles, item use, collectibles, and humor in a sci-fi facility setting. However, that evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is explicitly not puzzle-heavy, does not show clear platforming emphasis, does not establish meaningful combat loops, and lacks the stronger authored action/setpiece profile needed for cinematic_action_adventure or the horror/combat grounding needed for action_horror, survival_horror, or psychological_horror. This appears closer to a light narrative exploration adventure/walking-adventure hybrid, which is not an available archetype in the allowed list.

The narrative copy also emphasizes a story-led journey across countries with a Rider and trusted Rathalos, supporting a campaign-driven heroic adventure structure. Evidence for exact perspective, combat format, and more detailed structural facets is limited, so those fields are left blank rather than inferred too aggressively.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a semi-open world puzzle adventure centered on combining magical runes to create spells, exploring interconnected areas, solving environmental puzzles with multiple solutions, and pursuing a branching mystery narrative. That evidence is strong for a systemic puzzle-adventure identity, but the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly cover this profile. The strongest supported traits are semi-open exploration, environmental puzzle solving, authored branching narrative, and systemic spell-based problem solving; combat is not described as a primary loop, and the evidence does not firmly establish a supported archetype such as open_world_action_adventure, western_narrative_rpg, or immersive_sim_action. Because the game's core gameplay identity falls between taxonomy options and forcing a combat- or RPG-led archetype would misclassify it, rejection is more appropriate than a weak accept.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame Parkour Labs as a movement-centric 3D parkour/platforming game built around speed, flow, precision, chaining techniques, discovering shortcuts, and unlocking new challenges across stylized surreal environments. The gameplay identity is strongly non-combat and mastery-driven, which aligns better with precision_platformer than action_platformer. Level/route unlocking and secret discovery provide a lighter secondary resemblance to 3d_collectathon, but the core loop is skillful traversal rather than collection. Multiplayer/PvP is supported by store metadata, though the main descriptive copy emphasizes single-player challenge runs and mastery. VR support introduces a first-person play option, while the broader labeling and non-VR presentation support a third-person 3D platformer identity overall.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: First-person parkour challenge game about flowing through obstacle spaces, maintaining momentum, and mastering routes.

similar_games.why_similar: Arena-based survivor action with hero selection, fantasy attacks, rapid build crafting, and heavier active combat spectacle.

similar_games.title: Halls of Torment
similar_games.why_similar: Fantasy horde-survival runs focused on surviving waves, collecting loot and upgrades, and building stronger characters over repeated attempts.

similar_games.title: Death Must Die
similar_games.why_similar: Survivors-style runs with ARPG-flavored hero progression, randomized boons, treasure pickups, and dense fantasy enemy swarms.

similar_games.title: Brotato
similar_games.why_similar: Another highly replayable survivors-like centered on quick sessions, character variety, run-based upgrades, and surviving escalating waves.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a fast-paced 'survivor-style action roguelite' built around quick replayable runs, endless monster waves, hero unlocks, random power-ups, and build synergies in fantasy arenas. Metacritic also labels it a third-person shooter, but the descriptive copy emphasizes a broader survivor/bullet-heaven style action loop rather than any supported taxonomy archetype here. The closest allowed archetype would be co_op_action_roguelite, but there is no evidence of co-op. Shooter archetypes such as arena_fps, military_fps, hero_shooter, loot_shooter, or third_person_cover_shooter are not well supported by the actual loop description. Because the game's apparent core identity does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype and forcing a fit would be misleading, rejection is appropriate.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Definitive wave-survival roguelite built on short runs, escalating hordes, meta-unlocks, and synergistic power-up builds.
Arena-based survivor action with hero selection, fantasy attacks, rapid build crafting, and heavier active combat spectacle.
Fantasy horde-survival runs focused on surviving waves, collecting loot and upgrades, and building stronger characters over repeated attempts.
Survivors-style runs with ARPG-flavored hero progression, randomized boons, treasure pickups, and dense fantasy enemy swarms.
Another highly replayable survivors-like centered on quick sessions, character variety, run-based upgrades, and surviving escalating waves.

title: Anarchy Road
anchor_summary: Co-op-friendly arcade vehicular combat/racing: short match-based driving sessions built around speed, weapons, hazards, and chaotic multiplayer rather than simulation.

similar_games.title: BlazeRush
similar_games.why_similar: Top-down arcade combat racing with fast rounds, weapons, hazards, and strong local/party multiplayer appeal.

similar_games.title: Obliteracers
similar_games.why_similar: Chaotic multiplayer vehicle battles focused on arcade handling, eliminations, traps, and party-friendly couch play.

similar_games.title: Cel Damage HD
similar_games.why_similar: Vehicular combat built around cartoonish weapons, arena/race-style events, and pick-up-and-play local multiplayer.

similar_games.title: GRIP: Combat Racing
similar_games.why_similar: High-speed arcade racing fused with combat tools and aggressive track hazards, emphasizing destructive race flow over sim realism.

similar_games.title: Twisted Metal: Black
similar_games.why_similar: A recognizable vehicular-combat comp for weaponized cars, explosive action, and boss-style combat emphasis, though less race-driven.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text gives concrete gameplay, not just genre tags: it explicitly calls Anarchy Road a 'Post-Apocalyptic action driving game' where four heroes 'race against the clock,' 'battle bosses,' 'blast enemies,' and 'dodge traps.' Combined with racing/sports labels and co-op/split-screen categories, this supports an arcade-racer identity with strong vehicular combat and boss-focused setpiece encounters rather than a realistic racer or party game.
